channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.description:

  3. SpaceX launches 27 Starlink satellites on Falcon 9 rocket from Vandenberg  Spaceflight Now
  4. SpaceX stock in focus as launch, Starlink business hit new milestones  Yahoo Finance
  5. By mid-2026 a single company, SpaceX, had more than 10,000 Starlink satellites circling the Earth — roughly two of every three active satellites in orbit now belong to one man's network  Space Daily

item.title: Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman - The New York Times
item.description:
1. Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman  The New York Times

  3. 'Silly sprinklers' put in reverse to further unravel decades-old physics puzzle  Phys.org
  4. NYU Researchers Identify Physical Mechanisms Behind Reverse Sprinkler Motion  geneonline.com
  5. Feynman’s Famous Physics Problem of a Sprinkler Running in Reverse has a Surprising New Solution  The Debrief

item.description:

  3. Roman telescope may spot distant star-shredding black holes  AOL.com
  4. Ancient Black Holes Will Shred Stars Twice Weekly Under Roman Space Telescope  Tech Times
  5. NASA prepares to launch the Nancy Grace Roman Telescope, transforming the understanding of the cosmos and discovering exoplanets  Noticias Ambientales

item.title: Titan holds hundreds of times more liquid hydrocarbons than every known oil and gas reserve on Earth combined, yet you couldn't light a single drop — its air is nitrogen and methane, with almost no oxygen, so a campfire is physically impossible - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Titan holds hundreds of times more liquid hydrocarbons than every known oil and gas reserve on Earth combined, yet you couldn't light a single drop — its air is nitrogen and methane, with almost no oxygen, so a campfire is physically impossible  Space Daily
  2. Saturn’s Largest Moon May Hold the Resources for a Space Colony  SciTechDaily
  3. On Titan the mountains are made of water — frozen so hard at nearly minus 180 degrees that it behaves like rock, while the seas are liquid natural gas and the black beaches are soot drifting down from the sky  Space Daily
  4. Titan has rivers, rainfall, lakes and a slow hydrological cycle exactly like Earth's, except that every drop of it is liquid methane and the rock the rivers run over is water ice  Space Daily
  5. Titan, the interplanetary gas station - News  Techno-Science.net

item.title: Cutting the Tail of a Photon - American Physical Society
item.description: Cutting the Tail of a Photon  American Physical Society

item.title: What does it mean to be ‘quantum’? A physicist explains the basics behind Einstein’s spooky actions at a distance - The Conversation
item.description:
1. What does it mean to be ‘quantum’? A physicist explains the basics behind Einstein’s spooky actions at a distance  The Conversation
  2. Rethinking reality: Is the entire universe a single quantum object?  New Scientist
  3. Quantum logic: It’s simpler to be two things at once  New Scientist
  4. How quantum theory says we can never see a complete picture of reality  New Scientist

item.title: Beneath Europa’s fractured ice may be an ocean containing more liquid water than all of Earth’s oceans combined, kept warm in complete darkness by the gravitational pull of Jupiter - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Beneath Europa’s fractured ice may be an ocean containing more liquid water than all of Earth’s oceans combined, kept warm in complete darkness by the gravitational pull of Jupiter  Space Daily
  2. Scientists searching for alien life are looking ever further out, to Europa, a moon of Jupiter hiding an ocean beneath its ice that could resemble the dark seas where life survives on Earth  Space Daily
  3. A 2026 study found that Jupiter's ocean moon Europa may have a geologically quiet seafloor with little active faulting — meaning the vast hidden ocean long treated as our best bet for alien life may lack the very energy that life would need to survive  Space Daily
  4. Europa, one of Jupiter’s moons, is sealed beneath a shell of ice, but the ocean hidden below may contain twice as much liquid water as every ocean on Earth combined — and Jupiter’s gravity may have kept it liquid for billions of years  Space Daily

  3. Jersey Skies: Two asteroid flybys  Jersey's Best
  4. Nasa knows how to deflect an asteroid. Can Japan’s Hayabusa2 pull it off?  South China Morning Post
  5. ☄️ A peanut-shaped near-Earth asteroid seen up close  Techno-Science.net

item.title: Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future  Gizmodo
  2. Around 252 million years ago, volcanoes across what is now Siberia erupted repeatedly for more than a million years, releasing perhaps 100,000 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide and helping wipe out roughly 90 per cent of marine species in the deadliest mas  Space Daily
  3. The Permian Analogy: Climate Literacy and the Urgency We Cannot Grasp  Countercurrents
  4. Scientists may have solved the 252 million-year-old mystery behind Earth's greatest mass extinction  The Times of India
  5. Scientists finally confirm the cause of Earth’s greatest mass extinction  The Brighter Side of News

item.title: The Hubble Space Telescope reached orbit with a mirror ground to the wrong shape by a fraction of a human hair, and astronauts repaired the billion-dollar mistake by effectively giving it glasses - Space Daily

item.title: The James Webb Space Telescope found tiny “little red dots” that looked like impossibly compact galaxies — but astronomers now suspect many may be “black hole stars,” young black holes wrapped in gas so dense that the material falling into them produces - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The James Webb Space Telescope found tiny “little red dots” that looked like impossibly compact galaxies — but astronomers now suspect many may be “black hole stars,” young black holes wrapped in gas so dense that the material falling into them produces  Space Daily
  2. A mysterious “red dot” spotted in space: are we witnessing a cosmic birth?  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  3. How the Webb Telescope is Influencing Astrophysicists’ Theories  Adafruit
  4. Backyard Black Holes  Astrobites
  5. Black holes buried in mysterious 'little red dot' galaxies could blast cosmic ghosts at Earth  Space

item.description:
1. Frame-dragging observations validate Einstein yet again  Phys.org

  3. Armenian Scientist Co-Authors Study Confirming Einstein’s Theory with Record Precision  Asbarez.com
  4. Scientists just confirmed Einstein’s greatest theory—again  Scientific American
  5. An orbiting disco ball gave Einstein’s theory its most precise test yet  Ars Technica

item.description: Can humans hibernate their way to Mars?  The Guardian

item.title: We tend to assume Earth is a rare cosmic fluke, but a full analysis of NASA's Kepler data found at least 300 million rocky, habitable-zone planets around Sun-like stars in the Milky Way alone — and that is the conservative count - Space Daily
item.description: We tend to assume Earth is a rare cosmic fluke, but a full analysis of NASA's Kepler data found at least 300 million rocky, habitable-zone planets around Sun-like stars in the Milky Way alone — and that is the conservative count  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists show that DNA can last for up to 50,000 years in Africa — much longer than previously thought - Live Science
item.description:
1. Scientists show that DNA can last for up to 50,000 years in Africa — much longer than previously thought  Live Science
  2. Scientists recover sub-Saharan Africa's oldest ancient animal DNA  Phys.org
  3. 50,000-year-old DNA from South African antelope rewrites limits of ancient DNA preservation  Archaeology News Online Magazine
  4. 🧬 Record: 50,000-year-old DNA discovered in Africa  Techno-Science.net
  5. Scientists show DNA can last for up to 50,000 years in Africa — longer than previously thought  MSN

item.title: Physicists create first room-temperature quantum material - Phys.org
item.description: Physicists create first room-temperature quantum material  Phys.org

item.title: Ultraviolet light uncovers the first known juveniles of a mysterious Jurassic fish family - Phys.org
item.description: Ultraviolet light uncovers the first known juveniles of a mysterious Jurassic fish family  Phys.org

item.title: Voyager 1 crossed the edge of the Sun's protective bubble and entered interstellar space, but instead of fading gently into the void it met a strange, porous boundary scientists had not anticipated - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 crossed the edge of the Sun's protective bubble and entered interstellar space, but instead of fading gently into the void it met a strange, porous boundary scientists had not anticipated  Space Daily
  2. Voyager 1 crossed into interstellar space in August 2012, yet it remains inside the gravitational reach of our Sun — true departure from the solar system, via the Oort Cloud, won't happen for 30,000 years  Space Daily
  3. The engineers who built Voyager knew they would almost certainly be dead before it left the solar system, and they built it anyway, a gift from people who would never see where their work would go  ScienceBlog.com
  4. In August 2012, Voyager 1 recorded a sudden surge in galactic cosmic rays while the surrounding magnetic field barely changed direction—an unexpected combination that revealed the boundary of interstellar space was more complicated than scientists had  Space Daily
  5. We tend to think Voyager 1 has already left the solar system, but it has only crossed beyond the Sun’s protective plasma bubble — NASA says it will take another 300 years to reach the inner edge of the Oort Cloud, and perhaps 30,000 years to pass beyond th  Space Daily

item.title: Astronomers uncover the earliest known signs of galaxy-building in action - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Astronomers uncover the earliest known signs of galaxy-building in action  Phys.org
  2. Not clumpy, chaotic but earliest galaxies were remarkably organised, discovery shows  The Indian Express
  3. The oldest nuclear disk changes our understanding of the evolution of galaxies  Universe Space Tech
  4. When did the first galaxies form? Earlier than we thought possible  New Scientist
  5. James Webb has caught at least five galaxies colliding about 800 million years after the Big Bang, flinging heavy elements out into space far earlier than any model predicted — evidence the early universe was already messy and mature when it should still ha  Space Daily

item.title: Your Dominant Hand Isn't Actually Hard-Wired, New Study Suggests - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Your Dominant Hand Isn't Actually Hard-Wired, New Study Suggests  ScienceAlert
  2. Your dominant hand may not be born-in: A study that taped people’s elbows shut found both sides quickly learned to write, hinting practice is what makes one hand better  The Times of India
  3. Why is one hand better than the other? Study has the answer  Tech Explorist
  4. Being able to write easily with your dominant hand may not be something you're born with, but rather the result of 'lifelong practice.'  GIGAZINE
  5. Dominant hand excels through practice rather than brain advantage  News-Medical

item.title: The engineers trying to save a fading Voyager had one chance to fire thrusters frozen and unused for 37 years, and when the signal finally returned they learned the impossible had worked - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The engineers trying to save a fading Voyager had one chance to fire thrusters frozen and unused for 37 years, and when the signal finally returned they learned the impossible had worked  Space Daily
  2. NASA's Voyager 1 Only Has Two Active Science Instruments Left After Latest System Shutdown  Hypebeast
  3. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  4. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
  5. ATLANTIC SKIES: How a spacecraft launched in 1977 is sending signals beyond our solar system  PNI Atlantic News

item.description: The Grand Tour and the Three-Body Problem — Journey to Neptune  Daily Kos

item.description: Every SpaceX Starlink satellite has to dodge a collision almost weekly, and experts fear the worst  Space

item.title: What is the Real Estate Value of the Neighboring Habitable Planet GJ 3378 b? - Avi Loeb – Medium
item.description:
1. What is the Real Estate Value of the Neighboring Habitable Planet GJ 3378 b?  Avi Loeb – Medium
  2. Harvard astrophysicist talks new Earth-like planet and possibility of atmosphere  Yahoo
  3. Astronomers have found a super-Earth just 25 light-years away — close enough by cosmic standards that it sits in the small category of worlds scientists consider genuinely worth searching for signs of life  Space Daily
  4. Alien life on nearby exoplanet more likely than previously thought  The Jerusalem Post
  5. Astronomers have spent decades searching nearby stars for another Earth, and now they may have found one just 25 light-years away  The Times of India

item.title: A large amount of the Universe is missing. Scientists think they may have found it - BBC Sky at Night Magazine
item.description:
1. A large amount of the Universe is missing. Scientists think they may have found it  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  2. Vera Rubin watched stars at the edges of galaxies moving far faster than visible matter could explain and found one of the clearest early signs that most of the universe is made of something we cannot see  Space Daily
  3. Strangely familiar: Is dark matter normal stuff in disguise?  New Scientist
  4. If dark matter is everywhere, why do some galaxies seem to contain none at all?  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  5. Most of every galaxy is made of matter nobody has ever seen, detected, or explained — and the clearest early proof came from an astronomer named Vera Rubin, who simply measured how fast the outer stars of galaxies were spinning  Space Daily

item.title: A new paper argues that Earth's gravity is a hidden anchor for consciousness — and that losing it in orbit loosens the mind in a way that echoes psychedelics - Space Daily
item.description: A new paper argues that Earth's gravity is a hidden anchor for consciousness — and that losing it in orbit loosens the mind in a way that echoes psychedelics  Space DailySee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w  Space Daily
  2. Academia Sinica - Research Findings | Earth’s mantle locally heats up our planet’s core  sinica.edu.tw
  3. Earth’s inner core is an iron-rich ball as hot as the surface of the Sun, kept solid only because the crushing pressure of the entire planet raises the temperature at which iron can melt  Space Daily
  4. The Earth's inner core grows by roughly a millimetre a year as molten iron freezes onto it, and that slow crystallisation releases the energy that stirs the outer core and keeps the planet's magnetic field alive  Space Daily
  5. Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year.  Space Daily

item.title: Ocean acidification emerging as a planetary signal linking today's carbon emissions to Earth's deep-time memory - Phys.org
item.description: Ocean acidification emerging as a planetary signal linking today's carbon emissions to Earth's deep-time memory  Phys.org

item.description: The big reason Einstein would never have used AI  Big Think

item.description:
1. New 3D-printed invisibility cloak could hide objects from heat sensors  Interesting Engineering
  2. New 3D thermal cloak hides objects from heat in any direction  Phys.org
  3. First-ever 3D thermal cloak created by U of I team  Yahoo
  4. Tech Tuesday: Scientists create heat cloaking device  WLNS 6 News
  5. 3D-printed thermal cloak makes objects invisible to infrared cameras  heise online

item.title: People stunned after seeing how fast the International Space Station actually moves as wild simulation shows it at ground level - supercarblondie.com
item.description: People stunned after seeing how fast the International Space Station actually moves as wild simulation shows it at ground level  supercarblondie.com

item.title: A satellite launched in 1958, no bigger than a grapefruit and silent since 1964, is still the oldest human-made object circling Earth — and it is expected to keep quietly orbiting overhead for another few centuries - Space Daily
item.description: A satellite launched in 1958, no bigger than a grapefruit and silent since 1964, is still the oldest human-made object circling Earth — and it is expected to keep quietly orbiting overhead for another few centuries  Space Daily

item.title: Is there a ruined civilization on Mars? A theoretical physicist runs the numbers | Reality Check - Yahoo
item.description: Is there a ruined civilization on Mars? A theoretical physicist runs the numbers | Reality Check  Yahoo

item.title: Dogs do not see the world in black and white: they see much as a red-green colourblind person does, in blues and yellows — reds and greens simply fade to murky browns and grey, but colour is very much there - Space Daily
item.description: Dogs do not see the world in black and white: they see much as a red-green colourblind person does, in blues and yellows — reds and greens simply fade to murky browns and grey, but colour is very much there  Space Daily

item.title: The Amygdala Predicts Teen Isolation Years in Advance - Neuroscience News
item.description:
1. The Amygdala Predicts Teen Isolation Years in Advance  Neuroscience News
  2. Can a Preteen’s Reaction to a Face Predict Their Future Social Lives?  Nautilus
  3. Teen brains can predict their social lives years later  Earth.com
  4. Adolescent Brain Responses to Faces Could Forecast Social Development  Bioengineer.org
  5. How an adolescent's brain reacts to faces may predict their social future  Medical Xpress

item.description:
1. World-first neutron lens brings sharp focus to structures inside materials and objects  Phys.org

item.title: A new stellar census strengthens the case for a 13.8-billion-year-old universe - Phys.org

item.title: Astronomers Found a Hidden Swarm of Space Junk Threatening Earth's Most Valuable Orbit - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Astronomers Found a Hidden Swarm of Space Junk Threatening Earth's Most Valuable Orbit  Gizmodo
  2. Space junk debris cloud discovered in high-traffic orbit 'is a potential minefield' for the costliest satellites  Space
  3. Tiny space debris threatens Earth's busiest satellite highway  Earth.com
  4. Hidden Debris in GEO Is a Wake-Up Call: Visibility Must Be Matched by Action  Astroscale
  5. Adapting Satellite Design to the Growing Space Debris Problem  AZoQuantum

item.title: A new smart coating could improve the cleanup of nuclear wastewater - Phys.org
item.description:
1. A new smart coating could improve the cleanup of nuclear wastewater  Phys.org
  2. Material filters tritium from nuclear wastewater at record speed  The Hindu

item.title: India reached Mars on its first attempt with a mission that cost less than many Hollywood films, becoming the first Asian nation to enter Martian orbit and the first country anywhere to succeed on its maiden try - Space Daily
item.description: India reached Mars on its first attempt with a mission that cost less than many Hollywood films, becoming the first Asian nation to enter Martian orbit and the first country anywhere to succeed on its maiden try  Space Daily

item.title: A park ranger lowered himself into an Australian canyon expecting to document an ordinary patch of rainforest and found a stand of trees that had been missing from the living world for millions of years - Space Daily
item.description: A park ranger lowered himself into an Australian canyon expecting to document an ordinary patch of rainforest and found a stand of trees that had been missing from the living world for millions of years  Space Daily

item.title: Found on the roadside with 12 kitten siblings, Woodstock is thriving just 2 weeks after adoption - Yahoo
item.description: Found on the roadside with 12 kitten siblings, Woodstock is thriving just 2 weeks after adoption  Yahoo
